While the work involves high-level strategic synthesis, the environment is a controlled office setting, and the human necessity is largely limited to client relationship management and final sign-off on high-stakes budget allocations.

rows.title: Administrative services and facilities managers
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical site management. While AI can significantly automate administrative tasks like recordkeeping, supply procurement, and energy pattern analysis, the role requires a physical presence to inspect facilities, oversee manual labor (janitors, repair workers), and manage real-world safety and security compliance.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the administrative and records management component can be digitized and automated, the facilities management aspect requires navigating unpredictable physical environments and managing complex, non-routine maintenance issues. The role carries high systemic accountability for safety, security, and regulatory compliance, necessitating human oversight to manage liability and lead personnel in volatile, real-world scenarios.

rows.title: Dental assistants
rows.old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical, hands-on tasks such as sterilizing instruments, assisting in surgeries, and providing direct patient care that requires human presence and manual dexterity. While AI can significantly automate the administrative and digital aspects of the role—such as scheduling, billing, and preliminary X-ray analysis—the majority of the workday is spent in the physical dental operatory where AI cannot currently replace human labor.
rows.new_rationale: While recordkeeping and scheduling are highly automatable, the core of the role involves physical, high-dexterity assistance within the unpredictable and sensitive environment of a patient's oral cavity. The necessity for human presence is high due to the requirement for patient comfort, empathy, and the physical coordination required to support a dentist in real-time, which remains difficult for current autonomous agents to replicate safely.

rows.title: Claims adjusters, appraisers, examiners, and investigators
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is heavily focused on information processing, document review, and data-driven decision-making, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. While physical inspections and interpersonal negotiations provide some protection, the BLS already projects a decline in employment due to AI's ability to analyze photos of damage and automate claim calculations.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role—the determination of claim validity and settlement amounts—is highly digitizable, as evidenced by the rapid adoption of AI for image analysis and automated decision-making. While physical site inspections involve some environmental unpredictability, the primary value is the synthesis of data into a financial decision, which carries significant systemic accountability that currently requires a human 'sign-off' for legal and ethical liability.

rows.title: Tellers
rows.old_rationale: Tellers face high exposure because their core tasks—processing routine financial transactions and verifying information—are digital in nature and highly susceptible to automation through AI and advanced self-service technology. While the physical handling of cash provides a slight buffer, the rapid shift toward mobile banking, AI-driven fraud detection, and interactive video kiosks is significantly reducing the need for human intervention in these roles.
rows.new_rationale: The core tasks of a teller—processing transactions, verifying identity, and balancing accounts—are almost entirely digital or can be fully automated by existing banking software and hardware. Because the work occurs in a highly controlled, predictable office environment and requires minimal complex synthesis, it is exceptionally vulnerable to replacement by autonomous agents and enhanced kiosks.

rows.title: Network and computer systems administrators
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is primarily digital and involves tasks like configuration, monitoring, and troubleshooting that are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and 'infrastructure as code' practices. While some physical hardware maintenance remains, the shift toward cloud computing and AI-powered network management tools is already leading to a projected decline in employment as fewer workers can manage larger, more complex systems.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role is entirely digital—configuring, patching, and managing software-defined infrastructure—making it highly susceptible to autonomous agents. While the work requires complex troubleshooting and systemic oversight, the physical environment is largely controlled, and the human element is primarily functional rather than relational, leaving systemic accountability as the main barrier to full automation.

rows.title: Personal financial advisors
rows.old_rationale: The core technical tasks of this role—analyzing market data, optimizing portfolios, and tax planning—are digital and highly susceptible to AI automation, as evidenced by the rise of robo-advisors. However, the occupation is protected by a significant interpersonal component, as clients often require human trust, emotional reassurance during market volatility, and complex ethical judgment that AI cannot yet fully replicate.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role—financial planning and asset allocation—is highly digital and executable by software, giving it high agentic potential. However, the role remains heavily anchored by the need for human trust, behavioral coaching, and legal accountability for fiduciary decisions, which limits the total displacement of the human advisor.

rows.title: Public relations specialists
rows.old_rationale: The core tasks of this occupation—writing press releases, drafting speeches, creating social media content, and monitoring public sentiment—are digital-first and align perfectly with the capabilities of Large Language Models. While high-level strategy and real-time interpersonal crisis management provide some protection, the vast majority of the daily work product can be generated or significantly optimized by AI, leading to high exposure and potential workforce consolidation.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role—press releases, social media strategy, and communication plans—is almost entirely digital and highly susceptible to AI automation. While the job requires strategic synthesis of public sentiment, the physical environment is highly controlled, and the human necessity is largely limited to managing trust and reputation, which can be increasingly delegated to sophisticated agents.

rows.title: Coaches and scouts
rows.old_rationale: While AI is significantly impacting the analytical side of the job—such as processing player data, optimizing game strategy, and analyzing opponent film—the core of the occupation remains deeply physical and interpersonal. Coaches must provide real-time motivation, physical instruction, and human leadership on the field, which cannot be replicated by digital tools.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can synthesize vast amounts of performance data for scouting and strategic planning, the core value of coaching relies on human-to-human mentorship, psychological motivation, and the management of team dynamics, which cannot be delegated to software. The physical environment of sports is inherently unpredictable and requires real-time, embodied human judgment that remains outside the scope of autonomous agent execution.

rows.title: Masonry workers
rows.old_rationale: Masonry is a highly physical trade performed in unpredictable outdoor environments that require manual dexterity, strength, and real-time sensory feedback. While AI and robotics may assist with blueprint analysis or specialized brick-laying machines in controlled settings, the core tasks of navigating muddy sites, handling heavy materials, and finishing surfaces remain resistant to digital automation.
rows.new_rationale: Masonry is a highly physical trade where the output is a permanent, structural modification of the physical world, making it extremely resistant to digital agentic execution. The work occurs in highly unstructured, volatile construction environments that require real-time physical adaptation, while the systemic accountability for structural integrity and safety remains a significant, non-delegatable human burden.

rows.title: EMTs and paramedics
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical intervention, manual dexterity, and real-time human presence in unpredictable environments, which are highly resistant to AI. While AI will significantly assist with peripheral tasks like EKG interpretation, diagnostic decision support, and automated documentation, the physical requirements of patient transport and hands-on emergency care remain essential human functions.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can assist in diagnostic synthesis, the core output of this role is physical intervention in highly volatile, unstructured environments where robotic dexterity and real-time physical navigation remain significant bottlenecks. The necessity for human presence is absolute due to the requirement for bedside empathy, moral decision-making in triage, and the non-delegatable legal liability inherent in life-or-death emergency medical care.

rows.title: Physical therapists
rows.old_rationale: The core of physical therapy involves manual manipulation, physical assistance, and real-time observation of human movement in a physical environment, which AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with administrative tasks, medical history review, and generating personalized exercise plans, the high level of physical dexterity and interpersonal empathy required limits its overall exposure.
rows.new_rationale: Physical therapy is fundamentally anchored in physical, tactile interaction with human bodies, making the agentic output potential extremely low as the 'product' is physical rehabilitation rather than digital data. The work requires high-level synthesis of biomechanical data and patient psychology, performed in unpredictable, high-stakes clinical environments where the human-to-human bond and legal liability for patient safety are non-delegatable.

rows.title: Heavy vehicle and mobile equipment service technicians
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, involving manual dexterity, strength, and real-world troubleshooting in unpredictable environments like farms and construction sites. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic software and predictive maintenance logs, it cannot perform the physical disassembly, repair, and reassembly of heavy mechanical components.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of this role is physical intervention in highly unstructured, volatile environments (e.g., field repairs on construction sites), which remains largely beyond the reach of current autonomous agents. While diagnostic software is increasingly used, the final execution requires complex physical dexterity and the ability to navigate unpredictable mechanical failures, coupled with high systemic accountability for the safety of heavy machinery.

rows.title: Career and technical education teachers
rows.old_rationale: While AI can significantly automate lesson planning, grading, and the delivery of theoretical knowledge, the core of this occupation involves supervising hands-on activities in physical environments like workshops and labs. Teaching students to safely use physical tools for trades like welding, culinary arts, or auto repair requires real-time human presence and physical intervention that AI cannot currently replicate.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can generate curriculum and instructional materials, the core value of a CTE teacher lies in the physical, real-time supervision of students handling dangerous tools and equipment in workshops, which requires human presence for safety and mentorship. The role is deeply rooted in the human-to-human pedagogical bond and the non-delegatable legal and ethical responsibility for student safety in high-stakes, unpredictable physical environments.

rows.title: Instructional coordinators
rows.old_rationale: Instructional coordinators perform high-level knowledge work including curriculum development, data analysis, and educational material review, all of which are digital-first tasks highly susceptible to AI augmentation. While the role requires significant interpersonal interaction for teacher coaching and stakeholder management, the core 'product'—curricula and instructional strategies—can be generated and optimized much faster using generative AI and advanced analytics.
rows.new_rationale: The core outputs—curriculum design, data analysis, and instructional materials—are highly digital and easily executable by AI agents. However, the role remains anchored by the necessity for human-to-human mentorship, political navigation within school systems, and the professional accountability required to implement educational standards in a sensitive, human-centric environment.

rows.title: Postsecondary education administrators
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is heavily centered on digital information processing, including reviewing applications, analyzing student data, managing budgets, and maintaining academic records—all tasks where AI can significantly increase productivity or automate routine workflows. While roles like deans and student affairs officers require high-level human judgment and interpersonal relationship management, the underlying administrative and analytical work is highly susceptible to AI integration and restructuring.
rows.new_rationale: Much of the administrative output (scheduling, record-keeping, data analysis) is highly digital and ripe for agentic automation. However, the role remains anchored by high ontological necessity, as student affairs, conflict resolution, and academic leadership require human empathy, moral authority, and institutional accountability that cannot be fully offloaded to software.

rows.title: Cost estimators
rows.old_rationale: Cost estimation is a data-intensive, digital-first occupation centered on analyzing blueprints, technical documents, and historical databases to predict costs. AI and advanced software are already driving a projected decline in employment by automating routine calculations and simulations, though human oversight remains necessary for complex site-specific variables and high-stakes negotiations.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role is a digital estimate or bid, which is highly susceptible to automation via BIM and predictive modeling software. While the job requires analytical synthesis, the primary value is data-driven calculation rather than human-to-human connection, and the physical environment is largely peripheral to the desk-based task of generating the final financial artifact.

rows.title: Web developers and digital designers
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is entirely digital, with core tasks like writing code (HTML, JavaScript), designing layouts, and creating prototypes being areas where AI has demonstrated high proficiency. AI tools can now generate functional code from natural language and create sophisticated visual mockups, significantly increasing individual productivity and potentially reducing the number of developers needed for standard projects.
rows.new_rationale: The output of this occupation is entirely digital, making it highly susceptible to autonomous agents that can generate, test, and deploy code and design assets. While the work requires creative synthesis and client communication, the environment is strictly controlled and digital, leaving little room for physical-world constraints that would impede full automation.

rows.title: Information security analysts
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is entirely digital, involving tasks like monitoring networks, analyzing vulnerabilities, and writing reports that are highly susceptible to AI automation. While AI increases the demand for security to counter AI-driven threats, it also significantly automates the core functions of threat detection, log analysis, and incident response, drastically increasing individual worker productivity.
rows.new_rationale: The role is highly agentic because the primary output—code, configuration, and security policy—is natively digital and executable by autonomous systems. While the work requires high-level cognitive synthesis to counter evolving threats, the environment is almost entirely virtual, and the core necessity for a human is limited to high-stakes accountability and strategic decision-making rather than physical or interpersonal presence.

rows.title: Computer network architects
rows.old_rationale: The core work of designing, documenting, and configuring networks is fundamentally digital and data-driven, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and optimization tools. While physical hardware deployment and complex stakeholder management provide a slight buffer, AI is rapidly advancing in automated network topology design, security configuration, and predictive troubleshooting, which will significantly increase individual productivity and restructure the role.
rows.new_rationale: The output of a network architect is almost entirely digital (configurations, topologies, and code), making it highly susceptible to autonomous execution via Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) and AI-driven network orchestration. While the work requires high-level synthesis to design complex systems, the physical environment is largely controlled (data centers), and the human necessity is primarily limited to stakeholder communication and high-level strategic accountability rather than physical presence.

rows.title: Library technicians and assistants
rows.old_rationale: A significant portion of this role involves routine information processing, cataloging, and answering reference questions, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. While physical tasks like reshelving books and interpersonal community programming provide some protection, the core digital and clerical functions are increasingly being handled by automated systems and AI-driven search tools.
rows.new_rationale: The vast majority of tasks performed by library technicians—cataloging, database management, and information retrieval—are highly digitizable and can be executed by autonomous agents. While there is a human-facing component in assisting patrons, the core operational value is informational and clerical, occurring in a highly structured, predictable indoor environment that requires minimal physical improvisation.

rows.title: Aircraft and avionics equipment mechanics and technicians
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and real-time troubleshooting in a physical environment, which provides a strong barrier against AI automation. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic capabilities, predictive maintenance, and the interpretation of flight data, the actual repair, assembly, and FAA-regulated physical inspections must still be performed by human technicians.
rows.new_rationale: While diagnostics and data analysis are increasingly automated, the final output requires complex physical manipulation of hardware in unstructured, high-stakes environments where robotic dexterity remains limited. The extreme systemic accountability—where human lives depend on the physical integrity of the repair—creates a non-delegatable legal and ethical burden that necessitates human oversight regardless of AI diagnostic capability.

rows.title: Occupational therapists
rows.old_rationale: Occupational therapy is a highly physical and interpersonal profession that requires real-time observation, manual manipulation of patients, and the assessment of physical environments like homes and workplaces. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks such as treatment planning, progress documentation, and medical history analysis, the core of the job involves hands-on rehabilitation and emotional support that cannot be replicated digitally.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can assist in designing treatment plans, the core output of an occupational therapist is physical, hands-on rehabilitation and environmental assessment that requires real-time, high-stakes physical interaction with patients. The necessity for human empathy, trust, and the legal/ethical liability of managing patient health outcomes makes this role highly resistant to full automation.

rows.title: Physical therapist assistants and aides
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical, hands-on work such as massage, stretching, and assisting patients with mobility, which AI cannot perform. While AI can streamline peripheral tasks like scheduling, documentation, and exercise plan generation, the requirement for real-time human presence and physical dexterity creates a strong barrier to automation.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of this role is rooted in physical, empathetic, and high-trust human interaction, which cannot be replaced by digital agents. While AI can assist with scheduling or data recording, the physical execution of therapy—requiring real-time adjustments to a patient's physical state and the liability associated with patient safety—remains a deeply human-centric and physically constrained endeavor.

rows.title: Meeting, convention, and event planners
rows.old_rationale: A significant portion of the role involves digital tasks such as logistics planning, vendor research, contract review, and communications, which are highly susceptible to AI automation and productivity gains. However, the core of the job requires physical site inspections, real-time on-site problem solving during events, and high-stakes interpersonal negotiation and relationship management, which provide a buffer against full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the logistical coordination, vendor communication, and scheduling can be automated via software agents, the role remains heavily dependent on human-to-human trust, emotional intelligence, and the ability to manage high-stakes, real-time social dynamics. The physical environment of events is inherently unpredictable, requiring on-site human judgment to resolve conflicts and manage unexpected crises that software cannot yet navigate.

rows.title: Airline and commercial pilots
rows.old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of high-level digital information processing and critical physical presence. While AI and advanced automation can handle navigation, flight planning, and system monitoring, the role requires real-time human intervention for physical emergencies, complex sensory awareness in unpredictable weather, and the legal/ethical responsibility of passenger safety in the physical world.
rows.new_rationale: While flight path navigation and aircraft operation are increasingly digital and automatable, the high-stakes, unstructured nature of emergency response and volatile physical environments (weather, mechanical failure) keeps the role physically grounded. The primary barrier to full automation is not the technical execution of flight, but the absolute systemic accountability for life-critical decisions that currently requires a human legal entity to hold liability.

rows.title: Dentists
rows.old_rationale: The core of dentistry involves highly precise physical procedures, manual dexterity, and real-time patient interaction in a clinical setting, which are resistant to AI automation. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic accuracy through X-ray analysis and streamline administrative practice management, the physical requirement of performing surgery and restorative work creates a strong barrier to high exposure.
rows.new_rationale: While diagnostic and planning aspects of dentistry are increasingly digital, the core value remains high-stakes, invasive physical intervention in a highly variable, living biological environment. The requirement for real-time manual dexterity, patient management, and absolute legal/ethical liability for irreversible physical outcomes makes full automation of the 'final step' extremely unlikely in the foreseeable future.

rows.title: Librarians and library media specialists
rows.old_rationale: Librarianship is fundamentally about information retrieval, organization, and research—domains where LLMs and AI search engines excel. While the physical presence in schools and public libraries provides a buffer through community programming and interpersonal support, the core technical tasks of cataloging, database management, and reference assistance are highly exposed to AI automation and displacement.
rows.new_rationale: The core function of a librarian—information retrieval, curation, and synthesis—is highly susceptible to automation, as the output is almost entirely digital or informational. While the role involves human-centric tasks like teaching and community programming, the physical environment is highly structured and predictable, allowing AI agents to handle the vast majority of research and administrative workflows.

rows.title: Surgical assistants and technologists
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, requiring manual dexterity, sterile technique, and real-time presence in an operating room. While AI and robotics can assist with surgical precision or inventory management, the role remains centered on hands-on patient care and physical equipment handling that cannot be digitized.
rows.new_rationale: While surgical technologists perform structured tasks like instrument passing, the role requires high-stakes, real-time adaptation to the unpredictable physical environment of an active surgical field. The necessity for human presence is driven by the requirement for immediate, non-delegatable accountability for patient safety and the complex, high-consequence nature of physical intervention in a living human body.

rows.title: Fundraisers
rows.old_rationale: Fundraising involves significant digital work including donor research, data analysis, and the creation of promotional messaging, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While major gift solicitation and event management require high-level interpersonal skills and physical presence, the majority of the administrative, analytical, and communication-based tasks are moving toward AI-driven workflows.
rows.new_rationale: While the logistical and analytical components of fundraising (donor research, campaign management, and digital outreach) are highly susceptible to AI automation, the core value proposition remains the cultivation of human relationships and trust. The necessity for a human to provide the emotional labor and social validation required to secure major gifts or planned giving creates a significant barrier to full agentic replacement, despite the high potential for AI to execute the underlying administrative and communication tasks.

rows.title: Electrical power-line installers and repairers
rows.old_rationale: The core work is highly physical, involving climbing towers, operating heavy machinery, and manual repair in unpredictable outdoor environments. While AI can assist with remote monitoring and diagnostic data analysis, it cannot perform the high-risk physical labor required to install or repair high-voltage infrastructure.
rows.new_rationale: The output of this job is physical manipulation of high-voltage infrastructure, which remains largely beyond the reach of current autonomous agents due to the extreme environmental unpredictability of storm-damaged, unstructured outdoor sites. While diagnostic tasks are cognitive, the primary value lies in the high-stakes physical execution and the non-delegatable legal and safety accountability for maintaining critical public utility systems.

rows.title: Sheet metal workers
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and on-site installation in unpredictable construction environments, which are highly resistant to AI. While AI and computer-controlled machinery (CADD, lasers, and automated presses) are increasingly used in the fabrication phase, the majority of the work—including installation, maintenance, and complex physical maneuvering—cannot be performed by digital tools.
rows.new_rationale: While fabrication can be automated, the installation of sheet metal in construction involves highly unstructured, volatile physical environments that require real-time navigation of cramped, non-standard spaces. The physical nature of the work and the necessity of manual dexterity in unpredictable site conditions make it resistant to full agentic replacement, despite the existence of digital design tools.

rows.title: Computer programmers
rows.old_rationale: The core tasks of writing, testing, and debugging code are entirely digital and align perfectly with the strengths of Large Language Models, which are already highly proficient in these domains. The BLS specifically projects a decline in this occupation due to automation and AI, as these tools allow fewer workers to produce the same volume of code and shift higher-level logic to software developers.
rows.new_rationale: Computer programming is a purely digital discipline where the output is executable code, making it highly susceptible to full automation by AI agents. Because the work occurs entirely within controlled digital environments and lacks a requirement for physical presence or human-to-human emotional labor, the role faces extreme displacement pressure.

rows.title: Electrical and electronics installers and repairers
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical manipulation of hardware, manual troubleshooting in unpredictable environments, and the use of hand tools, which are highly resistant to AI automation. While AI can significantly enhance diagnostic software and predictive maintenance schedules, the physical acts of disassembling, repairing, and reassembling complex electrical components require human dexterity and presence.
rows.new_rationale: While diagnostic software assists in identifying faults, the final value of the job is a physical repair in unstructured, often cramped or volatile environments that remain highly resistant to current robotics. The high systemic accountability score reflects the critical nature of power grids and transportation systems, where human oversight is required for safety and liability in high-stakes physical interventions.

rows.title: Oil and gas workers
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves heavy physical labor, equipment maintenance, and manual operation in unpredictable outdoor environments, which provides a strong buffer against AI. However, the industry is increasingly adopting automated drilling technologies and remote monitoring systems that improve productivity, meaning fewer human workers may be required to achieve the same output.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of this occupation is physical labor in highly volatile, unstructured environments (offshore rigs, remote sites) where robotic dexterity and mobility currently struggle to replicate human adaptability. While the cognitive tasks are increasingly automated, the physical execution and the high-stakes safety accountability required in hazardous, non-standardized environments necessitate a human presence that is difficult to replace with current agentic software.

rows.title: Compensation, benefits, and job analysis specialists
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital and data-driven, involving core tasks like market research, cost analysis, and report generation that align perfectly with AI's strengths. AI can automate the drafting of job descriptions, perform complex compensation benchmarking, and monitor regulatory changes, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the need for manual data processing.
rows.new_rationale: The core outputs of this role—salary structures, benefits administration, and job classifications—are entirely digital and data-driven, making them highly susceptible to automation by AI agents. While the work requires complex synthesis of labor laws and market trends, it occurs in a highly controlled, predictable office environment where the human element is primarily limited to communication and policy interpretation rather than essential physical or emotional presence.

rows.title: Public safety telecommunicators
rows.old_rationale: The core tasks of this role—receiving digital inputs (voice, text, video), processing information according to protocols, and dispatching resources—are highly susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. While the high-stakes nature and need for human empathy provide a temporary buffer, AI's ability to transcribe, translate, and prioritize emergency calls in real-time will significantly restructure the occupation and increase individual worker productivity.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role—dispatching resources and logging data—is highly digitizable and executable by AI agents, which are already capable of processing multi-modal inputs and optimizing logistics. However, the role maintains high scores in ontological necessity and systemic accountability because the human-to-human connection is critical for de-escalating panic, and the legal/ethical liability for life-or-death decision-making remains a significant barrier to full automation.

rows.title: Skincare specialists
rows.old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical, hands-on treatments like facials, massages, and hair removal that require real-time human presence and manual dexterity. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like skin analysis, product recommendations, and business management, it cannot replace the tactile and interpersonal nature of the primary service.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of this occupation is rooted in physical, tactile interaction and the human-to-human service experience, which cannot be digitized or automated by software. While AI can assist in product recommendations, the actual delivery of treatments requires human dexterity and the maintenance of a trust-based, empathetic physical environment, making it highly resistant to agentic replacement.

rows.title: Dietitians and nutritionists
rows.old_rationale: The core tasks of analyzing nutritional data, interpreting research, and creating personalized meal plans are highly digital and data-driven, making them susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. However, the role requires significant interpersonal counseling, empathy, and clinical judgment in medical settings, which provides a buffer against full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can generate highly personalized nutritional plans and analyze complex health data (high agentic potential), the core value of the role relies heavily on human-to-human behavioral coaching, empathy, and the ability to navigate the psychological barriers clients face when changing long-term habits. The work occurs in relatively controlled clinical or office environments, but the practitioner carries significant legal and ethical accountability for medical outcomes, necessitating a human in the loop for final clinical judgment and liability.

rows.title: Environmental scientists and specialists
rows.old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, including data analysis, report writing, and regulatory compliance, which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. However, the role is anchored by a physical component involving fieldwork, site inspections, and laboratory sample analysis that AI cannot currently replicate. AI will likely serve as a powerful tool for modeling environmental impacts and drafting technical documents, increasing individual productivity while leaving the physical data collection and stakeholder relationship management to humans.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the data analysis and reporting can be automated, the role requires navigating unstructured, volatile physical environments to collect ground-truth data that AI cannot yet reliably replicate. The high score in systemic accountability reflects the critical legal and ethical weight of environmental compliance and public health decisions, which require human professional judgment that cannot be fully delegated to an autonomous agent.

rows.title: Water transportation workers
rows.old_rationale: The core of the work is physical and requires real-time presence in unpredictable maritime environments, involving manual tasks like equipment maintenance, cargo handling, and emergency response. While AI can assist with navigation, fuel optimization, and predictive maintenance for engineers, the high-stakes physical safety requirements and the need for human intervention in complex docking or mechanical failures limit overall exposure.
rows.new_rationale: While navigation and engine monitoring are increasingly automatable, the physical reality of maritime operations—navigating volatile weather, managing mechanical failures in isolated environments, and performing manual deck maintenance—remains highly resistant to full agentic replacement. The high score in systemic accountability reflects the non-delegatable legal and safety responsibility of captains and engineers for life-altering outcomes in high-stakes, unpredictable physical environments.

rows.title: Film and video editors and camera operators
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical camera operation and digital video editing. While camera operators have a physical barrier to AI due to the need for on-site equipment management and real-world positioning, editors work entirely in digital environments where AI is rapidly automating tasks like color grading, sound syncing, and even rough-cut generation. The rise of generative video and AI-driven post-production tools significantly increases the productivity of each worker, potentially reducing the total number of editors needed for complex projects.
rows.new_rationale: The output of this profession is almost entirely digital, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven editing and automated cinematography. While high-level creative synthesis is required for artistic direction, the physical component of camera operation is increasingly being offloaded to robotic systems and drones, reducing the necessity for human presence in volatile environments.

rows.title: Property appraisers and assessors
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves analyzing data, comparing market trends, and generating reports, all of which are highly susceptible to Automated Valuation Models (AVMs) and AI-driven data analysis. While physical site inspections and the need to defend assessments in public hearings provide a buffer, the shift toward 'desktop appraisals' and AI-assisted mass assessments significantly reduces the human labor required per valuation.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role is a digital valuation report, which is increasingly generated by automated valuation models (AVMs) and software, giving it high agentic potential. While site visits involve physical inspection, the primary barrier to full automation is the systemic accountability required for legal and tax-related liability, where a human must ultimately sign off on the valuation to defend it in public or legal forums.

rows.title: Computer hardware engineers
rows.old_rationale: The core work of designing schematics, analyzing circuits, and testing components is increasingly performed using digital Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools that are highly susceptible to AI-driven optimization and automation. While there is a physical component to testing and manufacturing oversight, the vast majority of the engineering lifecycle is digital and benefits significantly from AI's ability to optimize complex systems and generate code/hardware descriptions.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role—schematics, circuit designs, and hardware specifications—is highly digital and increasingly executable by AI-driven EDA (Electronic Design Automation) tools. While the work requires high-level cognitive synthesis to solve complex integration problems, it occurs in highly controlled, predictable laboratory or office environments, minimizing the need for human physical intervention or emotional presence.

rows.title: Community health workers
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation is built on trust, cultural sensitivity, and physical presence within specific communities, which AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like data collection, report writing, and resource matching, the primary duties involve home visits, hands-on advocacy, and real-time interpersonal coaching for vulnerable populations.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can assist with data collection and resource navigation, the core value of this role is built on human-to-human trust, cultural sensitivity, and physical presence in unpredictable community environments. The work requires navigating complex, non-standardized social and physical settings where the human connection is the primary mechanism for achieving health outcomes, making it highly resistant to full agentic replacement.

rows.title: Civil engineering technologists and technicians
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical field work. While AI and advanced CAD software are significantly automating drafting, cost estimation, and data analysis tasks, the role still requires physical site visits, material testing, and real-world inspections that AI cannot currently perform.
rows.new_rationale: A significant portion of the work involves digital drafting, data analysis, and reporting, which are highly susceptible to automation. However, the role requires physical site inspections and material testing in unstructured, volatile construction environments where human sensory input and physical presence are currently required to verify real-world conditions against digital models.

rows.title: Chiropractors
rows.old_rationale: The core of chiropractic work is highly physical, requiring manual dexterity for spinal adjustments and real-time physical assessment of a patient's musculoskeletal system. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like medical imaging analysis, diagnostic suggestions, and administrative business management, it cannot replace the essential hands-on therapeutic interventions that define the profession.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of chiropractic care is rooted in physical, manual manipulation of the human body, which requires high-stakes tactile feedback and real-time adjustment that cannot be offloaded to digital agents. While diagnostic synthesis is complex, the necessity for human touch, patient trust, and the profound legal and ethical liability associated with spinal manipulation creates a high barrier to full automation.

rows.title: Sales engineers
rows.old_rationale: Sales engineers perform high-level knowledge work that is increasingly digital, including technical presentations, product customization, and troubleshooting. While AI can automate technical documentation, demo generation, and data-driven customer insights, the role's heavy reliance on complex interpersonal relationships, negotiation, and trust-building provides a significant buffer against full automation.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of a sales engineer is highly digital and communicative, making it prime for AI-driven technical demonstrations and proposal generation. However, the role retains a high ontological necessity because the 'sale' relies on building human trust, navigating complex organizational politics, and providing the social reassurance required for high-stakes B2B procurement decisions.

rows.title: Surveyors
rows.old_rationale: Surveying is a hybrid occupation that combines significant physical fieldwork with digital data processing. While AI and automation (via drones and GIS) are rapidly increasing productivity in the mapping and analysis phases, the core requirements of physical site visits, navigating rugged terrain, and providing legally certified human testimony in court create a substantial barrier to full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While data collection and mapping are increasingly automated via drones and robotic stations, the job requires navigating highly unstructured, rugged physical environments that remain difficult for autonomous agents to traverse reliably. The high score in systemic accountability reflects the fact that surveyors provide legally binding certifications for property boundaries, a role that requires human professional liability and ethical judgment that cannot be delegated to software.

rows.title: Tour and travel guides
rows.old_rationale: While AI can automate the digital aspects of the job—such as researching history, planning itineraries, and providing multilingual translations—the core of the role requires physical presence and real-time human interaction. Guides must physically escort groups through unpredictable environments, manage group dynamics, and provide a personal, empathetic experience that digital tools cannot fully replicate.
rows.new_rationale: While digital information delivery can replace the factual component of a tour, the core value of the role is the human-to-human social experience, group management, and physical presence in unpredictable environments. The job requires real-time physical adaptation to weather and group dynamics that software cannot replicate, making the human element the primary product rather than a mere information delivery mechanism.

rows.title: Occupational therapy assistants and aides
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical assistance, manual therapy, and real-time human interaction, such as lifting patients and guiding motor skill exercises, which AI cannot perform. While AI can streamline administrative tasks like scheduling, documentation, and billing, the primary value of the role is rooted in physical presence and empathetic interpersonal support.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of this role is rooted in physical, empathetic, and high-stakes human interaction, such as assisting with patient mobility and providing emotional support, which cannot be digitized or delegated to software. While AI can assist with administrative tasks, the physical unpredictability of working with patients in varied health states and the non-delegatable legal and ethical liability for patient safety ensure that the human element remains central to the occupation.

rows.title: Craft and fine artists
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical craftsmanship and digital creation. While craft artists (potters, glassblowers) and sculptors have low exposure due to the physical nature of their work, digital artists and illustrators face very high exposure as AI can now generate high-quality imagery and complex designs from text prompts. The overall score reflects this split, as AI significantly impacts the commercial and digital segments of the field while leaving traditional physical mediums largely intact.
rows.new_rationale: While digital art and illustration have high agentic potential, the core of this occupation involves physical craft (sculpture, glassblowing, textiles) that requires complex, non-routine physical manipulation and material interaction that AI cannot currently execute. The value of 'fine art' is fundamentally rooted in human intent, cultural context, and the 'human touch,' making the ontological necessity of a human creator extremely high despite the low systemic accountability for the work's output.

rows.title: Lodging managers
rows.old_rationale: Lodging managers perform a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical site management. While AI can automate back-office tasks like revenue management, dynamic pricing, and staff scheduling, the core role requires physical inspections of facilities and high-stakes interpersonal conflict resolution with guests and staff that cannot be fully digitized.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the operational and financial management can be digitized and automated, the role remains anchored in the physical, unpredictable environment of hospitality where real-time conflict resolution and human-to-human service are primary value drivers. The necessity for a human presence to manage staff, handle high-stakes guest grievances, and provide moral authority in a physical space limits the total displacement potential of autonomous agents.

rows.title: Budget analysts
rows.old_rationale: Budget analysts perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving data analysis, financial modeling, and report writing—all areas where AI is highly capable. While the role requires human negotiation and communication with stakeholders, the core tasks of consolidating budgets, identifying discrepancies, and performing cost-benefit analyses are highly susceptible to automation and significant productivity gains through AI.
rows.new_rationale: Budget analysis is highly susceptible to automation because the output is almost entirely digital—reports, spreadsheets, and financial projections—which can be generated and executed by AI agents. While the role requires complex synthesis of financial data, the environment is strictly controlled and office-based, leaving the primary human necessity as the 'human-in-the-loop' for political accountability and the defense of recommendations before legislative bodies.

rows.title: News analysts, reporters, and journalists
rows.old_rationale: The core work product is digital (text, scripts, and multimedia), and AI can already automate significant portions of research, drafting, and editing. While physical presence for field reporting and the cultivation of human sources provide a protective barrier, the overall occupation faces high exposure as AI tools drastically increase individual productivity, potentially leading to further industry consolidation and fewer required roles.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this profession is digital information, which is highly susceptible to automation via generative AI agents capable of synthesizing data and drafting reports. While field reporting involves unpredictable physical environments and human-to-human trust for investigative access, the final delivery of news content is increasingly decoupled from the physical presence of a human reporter.

rows.title: Training and development managers
rows.old_rationale: This role is predominantly digital and knowledge-based, involving the creation of instructional content, data-driven needs assessments, and budget management—all areas where AI is highly capable. While the role requires significant human-centric leadership and stakeholder collaboration, AI can automate the generation of training materials, personalize learning paths, and analyze program effectiveness, substantially increasing the productivity of each manager.
rows.new_rationale: The core outputs of this role—curriculum design, budget management, and digital training delivery—are highly susceptible to automation via AI agents that can synthesize organizational needs into instructional content. While the role requires human-centric skills like leadership, mentorship, and navigating internal corporate politics, the technical execution of the training programs themselves is increasingly digital and agentic.

rows.title: Entertainment and recreation managers
rows.old_rationale: This role involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, such as budgeting, scheduling, and strategic planning, which is highly susceptible to AI optimization. However, the core of the job requires physical presence to inspect facilities, manage real-time emergencies, and provide high-touch interpersonal leadership and customer service in physical environments like parks and fitness centers.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the scheduling, budgeting, and logistical planning can be automated, the core value of this role lies in the 'human experience'—managing staff dynamics, resolving interpersonal conflicts, and ensuring customer satisfaction in physical, social environments. The necessity for real-time, on-site human judgment regarding safety, liability, and the intangible quality of leisure experiences creates a high barrier to full automation.

rows.title: Conservation scientists and foresters
rows.old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical field work, such as fire suppression, planting trees, and navigating difficult terrain, which provides a natural barrier to AI automation. However, a substantial portion of the role involves data analysis, GIS mapping, and regulatory compliance—tasks where AI can significantly enhance productivity and decision-making. While AI will reshape the information-processing aspects of the job, the requirement for real-world presence and manual intervention keeps exposure moderate.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can process GIS data and ecological models, the final output of this role is physical intervention in highly volatile, unstructured environments (e.g., fire suppression, land management) that require real-time, non-digital physical presence. The high systemic accountability stems from the legal and environmental consequences of land-use decisions and fire management, which require human liability that cannot be offloaded to an autonomous agent.

rows.title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, involving the analysis, organization, and validation of clinical data within computerized systems. AI is exceptionally well-suited for core tasks like data abstraction, classification coding, and generating reports from large datasets, which significantly increases the risk of automation and restructuring.
rows.new_rationale: The work is almost entirely digital, involving database management, coding, and reporting, which are highly susceptible to automation by AI agents. While the role requires high precision and adherence to ethical/legal standards regarding patient privacy, the physical environment is static and controlled, and the core tasks do not require a human presence to execute.

rows.title: Adult basic and secondary education and ESL teachers
rows.old_rationale: The core work product—language instruction, literacy training, and test preparation—is fundamentally digital and information-based, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven tutoring and translation tools. While the role requires significant cultural sensitivity and interpersonal motivation, AI can now provide personalized lesson plans, real-time feedback, and adaptive learning paths that significantly increase teacher productivity or replace routine instructional tasks.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role—instructional content, progress monitoring, and personalized learning plans—is increasingly digitizable and executable by AI agents, leading to high agentic potential. However, the role retains a high ontological necessity score because the effectiveness of adult education often relies on the human-to-human bond, cultural sensitivity, and the motivational support required to keep non-compulsory students engaged.

rows.title: Environmental science and protection technicians
rows.old_rationale: The core of this role involves physical fieldwork, such as collecting soil and water samples and inspecting facilities, which AI cannot perform. However, AI will significantly impact the digital aspects of the job, including data analysis, the preparation of reports and charts, and the modeling of environmental impacts.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the data analysis and reporting can be automated, the core of this role involves physical sample collection and inspection in unstructured, often hazardous field environments that remain difficult for autonomous agents to navigate reliably. The job carries significant systemic accountability regarding regulatory compliance and public health, requiring human oversight to validate findings and manage the liability associated with environmental safety decisions.

rows.title: Athletic trainers
rows.old_rationale: The core duties of athletic trainers—such as applying tape/braces, providing emergency first aid on the field, and performing manual rehabilitation—require physical presence and tactile skills that AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like injury analysis, record-keeping, and designing recovery protocols, the job remains fundamentally grounded in real-time human interaction and physical healthcare delivery.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of an athletic trainer lies in physical, real-time intervention and the human-to-human bond required for patient trust and emergency care, which cannot be digitized. While AI can assist in planning, the high-stakes, unpredictable physical environment of an injury on a field requires human physical presence and non-delegatable legal and ethical accountability for patient health.

rows.title: Actuaries
rows.old_rationale: Actuarial work is fundamentally digital, involving the analysis of large datasets, statistical modeling, and financial forecasting—all areas where AI and machine learning excel. While the profession requires high-level human judgment and the ability to explain complex risks to stakeholders, the core technical tasks of data compilation and probability estimation are highly susceptible to automation and significant productivity gains through AI.
rows.new_rationale: Actuarial work is highly digital and data-driven, making its outputs prime candidates for AI execution; however, the profession remains anchored by high-stakes systemic accountability, as actuaries are legally and ethically responsible for the solvency and regulatory compliance of financial institutions. While the cognitive synthesis is complex, the environment is almost entirely controlled and digital, meaning the primary barrier to full automation is not the difficulty of the task, but the requirement for human professional liability and the interpretation of complex, shifting regulatory frameworks.

rows.title: Credit counselors
rows.old_rationale: The core tasks of credit counseling—analyzing financial data, creating budgets, and explaining debt management options—are fundamentally digital and information-based, making them highly susceptible to AI automation. While the role requires interpersonal skills and emotional support for clients in financial distress, AI can already perform the complex calculations and personalized recommendation generation that constitute the bulk of the technical work.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this role—financial analysis, budgeting, and debt management plans—is entirely digital and highly susceptible to automation by agentic software. While the work environment is highly controlled and predictable, the role retains a significant human necessity component because clients often require emotional support, behavioral coaching, and trust-based guidance to navigate the stress of financial crisis.

rows.title: Fashion designers
rows.old_rationale: Fashion design is increasingly digital, utilizing CAD and graphics software for sketching, trend analysis, and virtual modeling—all areas where generative AI is rapidly advancing. While the physical aspects of selecting fabrics, creating prototypes, and overseeing production provide a buffer, the core creative and analytical tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and productivity gains.
rows.new_rationale: Fashion design relies heavily on digital CAD tools and trend synthesis, making a large portion of the creative output highly susceptible to AI generation and digital execution. While the physical prototyping and human-to-human brand connection remain important, the core design process is increasingly abstract and data-driven, reducing the necessity for human physical presence in the early and middle stages of production.

rows.title: Geoscientists
rows.old_rationale: Geoscientists have a balanced mix of physical and digital work. While AI is highly effective at analyzing seismic data, GIS mapping, and predictive modeling, the core of the job requires physical fieldwork, sample collection in remote environments, and laboratory testing that AI cannot perform. Consequently, AI will significantly enhance their analytical productivity but cannot replace the essential physical presence and real-world data gathering required for the profession.
rows.new_rationale: While geoscientists produce digital maps and reports, the core value is derived from high-stakes, non-routine synthesis of physical data collected in highly unstructured, volatile environments that remain difficult for autonomous agents to navigate. The high systemic accountability score reflects the legal and ethical weight of their findings, which often dictate multi-million dollar infrastructure projects, environmental safety, and resource extraction, where human professional judgment is required for liability.

rows.title: Elevator and escalator installers and repairers
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves highly physical, manual labor in unpredictable environments, such as elevator shafts and machine rooms, which provides a strong barrier against AI automation. While AI can significantly enhance diagnostic troubleshooting and predictive maintenance through computerized control systems, the actual installation, repair, and physical manipulation of heavy mechanical components still require a human presence. AI acts as a sophisticated tool for the technician rather than a replacement for the physical work.
rows.new_rationale: The core of this job is physical manipulation of heavy, complex machinery in unstructured, hazardous environments like elevator shafts and cramped crawlspaces, which remains highly resistant to current robotics. While the diagnostic aspect involves complex cognitive synthesis, the final output is a physical repair that requires human-level dexterity and liability, making it extremely difficult to delegate to autonomous agents.

rows.title: Air traffic controllers
rows.old_rationale: The core work is fundamentally digital and data-driven, involving the monitoring of radar screens and the processing of complex spatial information to ensure aircraft separation. While the high-stakes nature of safety and the need for real-time human communication with pilots provide a buffer, AI and automated systems (like NextGen) are already increasing productivity and handling more traffic per controller, leading to a very low projected growth in human employment.
rows.new_rationale: Air traffic control is highly susceptible to automation because the primary output is digital instruction (vectors, clearances) based on real-time data, which AI can process and execute with higher speed and precision than humans. While the environment is technically 'unstructured' due to weather and emergency variables, the system is heavily constrained by rigid protocols and radar-based monitoring. The high score in systemic accountability reflects the fact that, despite AI's technical capability, the legal and ethical burden of life-or-death decisions in aviation remains a non-delegatable human responsibility.

rows.title: Materials engineers
rows.old_rationale: Materials engineering is heavily reliant on digital simulation, data analysis, and computational modeling, areas where AI and machine learning are already accelerating material discovery and property prediction. While the role requires physical lab work and manufacturing oversight, a significant portion of the core value—designing alloys, analyzing failures, and writing technical reports—is increasingly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
rows.new_rationale: Materials engineering involves high-level computational modeling and simulation, allowing AI to handle much of the design and testing phase, though physical validation in labs remains a constraint. The role requires deep synthesis of multi-variable constraints (cost, durability, sustainability), but the primary barrier to full automation is the high-stakes systemic accountability for material failure in critical infrastructure, which requires human legal and ethical oversight.

rows.title: Chemical engineers
rows.old_rationale: Chemical engineering involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, including process simulation, CAD design, and data analysis, which are highly susceptible to AI-driven optimization. However, the role is anchored by the need for physical presence in laboratories and industrial plants to monitor equipment, ensure safety compliance, and troubleshoot hardware in the real world.
rows.new_rationale: While chemical engineers produce digital designs and process models that AI can increasingly generate, the physical implementation in volatile industrial environments requires complex, non-linear troubleshooting that remains difficult to automate fully. The high systemic accountability—where engineers are legally and ethically responsible for safety and environmental compliance in hazardous settings—creates a significant barrier to delegating final authority to autonomous agents.

rows.title: Radiation therapists
rows.old_rationale: The role requires a significant physical presence to position patients, operate heavy machinery, and provide empathetic interpersonal care during serious illness. While AI will heavily automate the digital aspects of the job—such as tumor localization, treatment planning, and dosage calculations—the physical and clinical safety requirements create a substantial barrier to full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While the operation of radiation equipment is highly technical and increasingly automated, the physical positioning of patients and the necessity of human oversight for safety and emotional support remain critical. The high systemic accountability score reflects the life-altering consequences of radiation errors, which necessitates a human-in-the-loop for both legal liability and real-time clinical judgment.

rows.title: Recreational therapists
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical presence, manual assistance, and real-time interpersonal interaction through activities like adaptive sports, dance, and community outings. While AI can significantly assist with digital tasks like patient assessment, treatment planning, and progress documentation, it cannot replace the physical and emotional labor required to lead therapeutic interventions in person.
rows.new_rationale: The core value of this role is rooted in human-to-human connection, empathy, and the physical presence required to guide patients through therapeutic activities, making it highly resistant to automation. While AI can assist in planning, the execution requires navigating unpredictable physical environments and managing the high-stakes emotional and medical needs of vulnerable patients, for which the therapist remains legally and ethically accountable.

rows.title: Audiologists
rows.old_rationale: Audiology is a clinical healthcare profession that requires physical presence for examinations, wax removal, and fitting hardware like hearing aids or cochlear implants. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic accuracy, signal processing in hearing devices, and automated testing, the core of the job involves interpersonal counseling, physical patient interaction, and complex clinical judgment that cannot be fully digitized.
rows.new_rationale: While diagnostic testing and data analysis are increasingly automated, the core of the role involves physical interventions (fitting devices, balance maneuvers) and high-stakes clinical judgment that requires human accountability. The necessity for empathetic, patient-centered counseling and the legal liability associated with medical diagnosis and treatment plans create a significant barrier to full agentic replacement.

rows.title: Calibration technologists and technicians
rows.old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant physical component, requiring manual dexterity to adjust hardware and presence in industrial or laboratory environments. While AI can automate data recording, scheduling, and the analysis of calibration deviations, the core tasks of physical inspection, maintenance, and hands-on equipment manipulation provide a natural barrier to full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While the diagnostic and reporting aspects of calibration are increasingly digital, the physical requirement to access, manipulate, and adjust hardware in diverse, often hazardous industrial environments limits agentic potential. The high systemic accountability stems from the fact that these technicians validate the precision of critical infrastructure and manufacturing equipment, where failure can lead to catastrophic safety or quality outcomes that require human legal and ethical oversight.

rows.title: Electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians
rows.old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical labor, including soldering, operating metalworking machinery, and repairing hydraulic systems, which provides a strong barrier against full AI automation. However, AI will increasingly assist with the knowledge-based aspects of the job, such as analyzing test results, interpreting complex schematics, and diagnosing faults in the very robotic systems these technicians maintain.
rows.new_rationale: While the occupation involves digital documentation, the core value is physical maintenance and repair of complex machinery in varied, often hazardous industrial environments, which limits agentic output potential. The work requires high-dimensional troubleshooting of physical systems that are often non-standardized or degraded, necessitating human dexterity and on-site judgment that remains difficult to fully automate.

rows.title: Cartographers and photogrammetrists
rows.old_rationale: The core tasks of collecting, analyzing, and visualizing geospatial data are fundamentally digital and highly susceptible to AI-driven automation. Computer vision and machine learning are already significantly faster than humans at interpreting satellite imagery, LiDAR, and survey data, while generative AI can increasingly automate the design and production of both static and interactive maps.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of this profession is digital geospatial data and visualization, which is highly amenable to automated generation via AI and remote sensing integration. While fieldwork introduces some physical variables, the vast majority of the work involves synthesizing complex datasets in a controlled office environment, leaving little requirement for human-to-human interaction while maintaining significant professional liability for the accuracy of critical infrastructure maps.

rows.title: Geological and hydrologic technicians
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital data analysis. While AI and automation (like drones and remote sensors) are increasingly handling data collection and mapping, the role still requires significant physical presence for equipment maintenance, manual sample collection, and laboratory testing that cannot be fully digitized.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the data analysis and reporting can be automated, the core of this role involves physical interaction with unstructured, remote, and volatile environments where robots currently struggle with navigation and sample integrity. The job requires significant physical presence and manual dexterity in unpredictable field conditions, limiting the immediate potential for full autonomous replacement despite the high digital output of the final reports.

rows.title: Boilermakers
rows.old_rationale: The core work is highly physical, involving manual assembly, welding, and repair in unpredictable and cramped environments like boilers and tanks. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint analysis or maintenance scheduling, the primary duties require human dexterity and physical presence that current AI and robotics cannot replicate in non-factory settings.
rows.new_rationale: Boilermaking is defined by high-stakes physical labor in unstructured, hazardous, and cramped environments that are currently beyond the reach of autonomous robotics. While the cognitive tasks involve technical skill, the primary barrier to automation is the physical execution of repairs in volatile, non-standardized industrial settings, coupled with the high systemic liability for structural integrity and safety.

rows.title: Orthotists and prosthetists
rows.old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical, hands-on work including patient examinations, manual measurements, and the physical fabrication or adjustment of medical devices. While AI and computer-aided design (CAD) can greatly enhance the diagnostic and design phases, the core requirements of real-time human interaction, physical dexterity, and clinical judgment in a healthcare setting provide a strong barrier to full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While AI can assist in the design and fabrication of devices, the core output is a physical, bespoke interface between a machine and a human body, which requires high-stakes physical adjustments that are difficult to automate. The role relies heavily on human empathy, trust, and the non-delegatable legal and ethical responsibility for patient mobility and safety, making it highly resistant to full agentic replacement.

rows.title: Anthropologists and archeologists
rows.old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital knowledge work. While AI can significantly automate data analysis, pattern recognition in artifacts, and report writing, it cannot replace the physical requirements of excavation, site surveying, and in-person ethnographic observation.
rows.new_rationale: While the analytical and reporting components of this role are increasingly susceptible to AI, the core value often resides in the physical, unstructured act of excavation and the nuanced, context-dependent interpretation of human behavior in remote or volatile environments. The necessity for human judgment in high-stakes cultural preservation and the physical unpredictability of fieldwork create significant barriers to full automation.

rows.title: Political scientists
rows.old_rationale: Political science is a purely digital knowledge-work occupation centered on data analysis, research, and writing, all of which are core strengths of generative AI and LLMs. While high-level strategic judgment and interpersonal networking remain human-centric, AI can now automate the collection of public opinion data, the drafting of policy briefs, and the forecasting of political trends, leading to significant productivity gains and a projected decline in total employment.
rows.new_rationale: The output of a political scientist is almost entirely digital—reports, forecasts, and policy analyses—which are highly susceptible to AI generation and execution. While the work requires high-level cognitive synthesis, it occurs in a highly controlled, desk-based environment, and the primary human necessity lies in the social and political trust required for advocacy rather than the technical production of the research itself.

rows.title: Hydrologists
rows.old_rationale: Hydrologists have a high exposure to AI because a significant portion of their work involves data analysis, computer modeling, and report writing—tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. However, the physical requirement of field work, such as collecting water samples and measuring streamflow in remote locations, provides a substantial buffer against full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While hydrologists produce digital models and reports that are highly automatable, the physical collection of data in volatile, unstructured natural environments remains a significant barrier to full autonomy. The high systemic accountability score reflects the critical nature of their work in public safety and infrastructure, where human oversight is required to validate AI-driven predictions before life-altering decisions are made.

rows.title: Medical dosimetrists
rows.old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves complex mathematical calculations, image analysis (CT/MRI), and digital treatment planning, all of which are domains where AI and machine learning excel. While there is a minor physical component involving patient positioning and equipment calibration, the vast majority of the work is digital information processing that is highly susceptible to automation and AI-driven optimization.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of a dosimetrist is a digital treatment plan, which is highly susceptible to AI automation given the computational nature of radiation physics. While the environment is highly controlled and predictable, the role carries extreme systemic accountability, as errors in radiation dosage have life-altering consequences, necessitating a human 'human-in-the-loop' for final verification and legal liability.

rows.title: Sociologists
rows.old_rationale: Sociology is a knowledge-intensive field where core tasks like data analysis, literature reviews, and report writing are highly susceptible to AI enhancement. While qualitative field research and interpersonal interviews provide a buffer, the shift toward digital data collection and AI-driven statistical modeling significantly increases worker productivity and reshapes the research process.
rows.new_rationale: Sociologists produce digital artifacts—reports, policy recommendations, and statistical models—that are highly susceptible to AI automation. While the work requires high-level cognitive synthesis to interpret complex social dynamics, the physical environment is largely controlled, and the primary value lies in the analytical output rather than a uniquely human physical presence.

rows.title: Agricultural engineers
rows.old_rationale: Agricultural engineering is a hybrid role that combines digital design and data analysis with physical site visits and hardware testing. While AI will significantly automate core tasks like CAD modeling, environmental simulation, and precision farming data analysis, the requirement for physical oversight of construction, equipment testing in the field, and real-world troubleshooting provides a buffer against full automation.
rows.new_rationale: While much of the design work is digital and increasingly automatable, the role requires navigating highly unstructured, volatile physical environments (farms, rural construction) where AI lacks the sensory-motor adaptability to troubleshoot real-world failures. The high score in systemic accountability reflects the critical nature of engineering safety, environmental regulation, and infrastructure integrity, which require human legal and ethical liability that cannot be offloaded to an autonomous agent.

rows.title: Geographers
rows.old_rationale: Geographers primarily perform digital knowledge work, including data analysis, GIS modeling, and report writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While some fieldwork exists, the core of the profession involves processing satellite imagery and census data—tasks where AI and machine learning are rapidly becoming more efficient than humans.
rows.new_rationale: The core output of a geographer—digital maps, spatial models, and analytical reports—is highly amenable to AI-driven automation via GIS and remote sensing integration. While fieldwork introduces some physical unpredictability, the primary value lies in the synthesis of complex datasets, a task where autonomous agents are increasingly capable of replacing human workflows, leaving only high-level policy and ethical oversight as the remaining human-centric components.
